Q1: I kept things pretty simple, honestly. I'd usually block out time on weekends and maybe two evenings during the week to go through materials. I'm a visual learner, so I'd download diagrams, charts, and slides early in the week and then work through them at my own pace. I didn't follow a strict hour-by-hour schedule, but I had a general rhythm that worked for me. Most of my actual studying happened away from the computer with printed materials spread out on my desk.
Q2: My pattern was fairly consistent week to week, though I'll admit deadlines did push me to adjust sometimes. I tried to front-load my work because I knew my anxiety would spike if I left things too late. Even though my attendance in live sessions was pretty low, I made sure my assignment work was always done well ahead of time. The consistency helped me manage my stress, even if it meant studying at odd hours when I felt calmer.
Q3: Usually it was checking if new assignments were posted or if there were any announcements about deadlines. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—I had a purpose each time. Sometimes I'd log in to download lecture slides or grab resources I needed for the week. If I'm being honest, I found the platform kind of tedious, so I tried to get what I needed and get out quickly.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward. I used it out of necessity—to submit work, download files, check grades. I guess around the third or fourth week I got into a rhythm of logging in on Mondays to grab materials for the week, but it never felt like a habit I enjoyed. It was more like a chore I had to complete before I could do my actual studying.
Q5: The video lectures were helpful because I'm a visual learner and could pause and rewatch sections that confused me. I also found the downloadable slides really useful since I could print them and annotate by hand. The discussion boards were okay for seeing how other students interpreted things, though I didn't post much myself. Anything visual or something I could take offline was valuable to me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums beyond occasionally reading them. Posting felt performative and didn't help me learn—I just got anxious about saying something wrong. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because they felt like busywork and I was already doing well on assignments. If something wasn't directly tied to an assessment, I probably didn't spend much time on it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept better. Visual resources like diagrams or worked examples were always worth it. If something felt vague or like it was just there to pad out the course, I'd lose interest quickly. I don't have a lot of resources to waste time on things that don't matter.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. Required ones had my full attention because they affected my grade. With optional stuff, I'd think 'is this really going to help me?' and usually the answer was no, or at least not enough to justify the time. I was already managing a lot with limited resources, so I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download everything I needed—slides, PDFs, diagrams—and then work from printed copies or my tablet without internet. I'd make handwritten notes, create my own visual summaries, and review those repeatedly. The LMS was just a delivery system for me; the actual learning happened at my desk with paper and highlighters.
Q10: Pretty much any time I was reviewing my printed notes or working through practice problems on paper. I'd spend hours going over visual summaries I'd created, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best study sessions were usually late at night with my notes spread out, no screens involved. The LMS would show I downloaded something once, but not the ten times I reviewed it afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, maybe twice if it was complex, then download the slides and never go back to the video. My studying was mostly from those downloaded materials. I'd print things out and carry them around, reviewing during commutes or breaks. One download could turn into weeks of offline study that the system would never capture.
Q12: There were definitely weeks where my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage with anything new. I'd feel overwhelmed even though I was doing well academically. Sometimes the boredom was the hardest part—the material wasn't challenging enough to keep me interested, but my anxiety made everything feel heavy anyway. During those times, I'd do the bare minimum online and just work from my existing notes.
Q13: My low resources meant I had to be really efficient with my time and energy. Fatigue was a big factor—I'd sometimes be too tired to log in and engage properly, so I'd just work offline instead. When multiple assignments piled up, I focused entirely on submissions and skipped any 'extra' engagement. The LMS felt like an energy drain, so I avoided it when I was already running low.
Q14: Honestly, yes. Sometimes I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered because I was too anxious or bored to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. There were times I logged in, clicked around aimlessly, and logged out having absorbed nothing. The activity was there, but the engagement wasn't.
Q15: I avoided discussion posts constantly, even though I knew participation mattered. The anxiety of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge was too much. I'd tell myself I'd post later, then never do it. Same with some of the optional readings—I knew they might help, but I kept pushing them off because the required work felt more urgent and less anxiety-inducing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for logging in. I'd check the LMS more frequently as deadlines approached, submit my work, then basically disappear until the next one. My whole engagement pattern was deadline-driven, even though I tried to complete things early to manage my anxiety. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have logged in much at all.
Q17: Feedback was important but also anxiety-inducing. When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep doing what I was doing. But waiting for feedback was stressful, and sometimes I'd avoid checking my grades for days because I was scared of disappointment. Once I saw I was doing well, I felt more confident but also less motivated to engage beyond what was necessary.
Q18: The structure was pretty standard, which worked okay for me. Having clear deadlines and organized modules made it easy to plan my offline study. But the course didn't really require much active participation, which meant I could get away with minimal LMS engagement while still performing well. The structure enabled my pattern of downloading and disappearing.
Q19: The visual resources made engagement easier—anything with diagrams or clear layouts worked well for me. What made it harder was the expectation of discussion participation; that triggered my anxiety without feeling academically valuable. Also, the platform itself wasn't very intuitive, which made me want to spend less time there. If the interface had been cleaner, I might have engaged more.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for something—maybe they're confused and clicking around trying to find answers, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually making progress. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Someone could be logged in for hours but paralyzed by anxiety or just not finding what they need. The numbers don't show the quality of that time.
Q21: That's basically my situation. I performed really well academically but my LMS activity was pretty low because I did most of my work offline. Once I had the materials downloaded, I didn't need the platform anymore. I think students who are organized and self-directed can succeed without constant online presence. The LMS is just one tool, and some of us use it minimally but effectively.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and how often, which gives a basic picture of activity patterns. But they miss everything that happens offline—the hours of review, the handwritten notes, the thinking that happens away from screens. They also can't capture the quality of engagement or whether someone was actually focused. It's like measuring cooking by how often someone opens the fridge.
Q23: Being engaged means actually thinking about the material and caring about understanding it, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively processing information—making connections, creating visual summaries, testing myself. It's not about how much time I spend online or how many posts I make. Real engagement is internal and doesn't always have external markers.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much mental energy it took me to complete even simple tasks. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes, the visual maps I created, or the way I'd review materials during moments the system couldn't track. It also wouldn't show that despite my boredom and low motivation, I genuinely cared about doing well—I just found my own way to achieve that outside the platform.
